input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: A building or infrastructure asset within the Architecture, Engineering, Construction, and Operations (AECO) domain, characterized by static models integrated with real-time operational data to create a virtual representation for simulation, analysis, and optimization across the building lifecycle from design to operation and refurbishment.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: Digital Twins provide real-time monitoring via data-driven insights, predictive analytics for forecasting system behavior, infrastructure optimization, and enhanced decision-making capabilities. Target users include architects, engineers, construction professionals, and facility operators across the building lifecycle stages from initial design to refurbishments and operational adaptations.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The framework integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange and leverages Unreal Engine for rich, interactive visualization, featuring standardized extension points to enable modular, service-oriented DT platforms.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: Computational models integrate geometric BIM representations with semantic data for Building Control Systems (BCS) components (sensors, actuators, controllers), using Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) to extract structured representations and generate deployable DT artifacts. Data sources include enriched BIM models and real-time physical building data, synchronized through continuous data exchange capabilities, with management via modular architecture supporting custom analytics services.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: Model-driven deployment framework with modular architecture and standardized extension points enables scalable integration of custom services; runtime configuration management allows dynamic adjustments without full redeployment, supporting scalability from individual components to enterprise-wide systems.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The lifecycle stages commence with device modeling in BIM environment where Building Control Engineers define sensors, actuators, and controllers to establish a unified semantic foundation. This is followed by Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) principles for extracting structured representations and automatic generation of deployable DT artifacts. The runtime phase includes real-time data exchange for continuous synchronization, interactive visualization for monitoring and control, and modular architecture enabling evolution through custom services and analytics integration.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The twinning process utilizes Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) to transform enriched BIM models into structured representations and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention. Evolution is managed via a modular architecture with standardized extension points, allowing integration of custom services and analytics to adapt to operational changes.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Decision support is enabled through interactive visualization interfaces for Facility Managers to monitor and control operations, supported by real-time data synchronization between physical and digital systems. Custom analytics services integrated via modular extension points provide actionable insights, though specific analytics methodologies or confidence measures for recommendations are not detailed.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The framework implements standardized extension points within its modular architecture to ensure interoperability and seamless integration of custom services and analytics. However, no specific industry standards (e.g., ISO, IFC) or compliance frameworks are explicitly referenced in the document.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately reflects the general definition of the physical system in the AECO domain described in the text, focusing on the integration of static models with real-time data for simulation and optimization. However, it represents a high-level definition rather than specific details of the 'Living Lab' case study or specific components (like lighting systems) mentioned elsewhere, making it correct but not exhaustive. Supported by quotes: 'virtual representation of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization', 'architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO) domain'.

The description is highly specific and grounded in the source text, accurately listing services like real-time monitoring, predictive analytics, and optimization. It correctly identifies the target users and lifecycle stages as described in the introduction. Supported by quotes: 'providing real-time insights, predictive analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities', 'enabling real-time monitoring, simulation, and life cycle-encompassing optimization', 'from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations during operation'.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source documents explicitly list the specific tools and enablers used. The abstract mentions: 'The framework integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange and leverages Unreal Engine for rich, interactive visualization.' It also specifies 'Building Information Modeling (BIM)' as the backbone. Since relevant information is present in the text, the claim that it is missing is incorrect.

block_2_judge.reasoning: The source documents mention 'real-time data exchange' and 'live synchronization' generally, but they do not provide specific details about synchronization frequencies, latency requirements, or temporal granularity needs as defined by the characteristic. Thus, stating 'Not in Document' is accurate regarding the specific technical details.

The source documents describe a framework for deploying Digital Twins and a specific case study (Living Lab) but do not mention multiple digital twin instances, centralized vs. decentralized architectures, or coordination mechanisms between twins. 'Not in Document' is correct.

The extraction accurately captures the system's orchestration and component integration. It is directly supported by the text which states: 'The framework integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange and leverages Unreal Engine for rich, interactive visualization' and features 'standardized extension points to enable modular, service-oriented DT platforms'.

The documents mention integration with Eclipse Hono and Ditto for connectivity, which is primarily for device-to-cloud (vertical) communication. They do not describe integration with external enterprise systems, interoperability standards for cross-system exchange, or integration patterns with other Digital Twins. 'Not in Document' is correct.

block_3_docs_retrieved:
TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extracted description is highly accurate and comprehensive, synthesizing the framework's use of BIM, MDE, and data integration as described in the text. It correctly identifies the integration of geometric BIM with semantic data for BCS, the use of MDE for artifact generation, real-time data sources, and the modular architecture. Quotes: 'model-driven framework for scalable DT deployment, grounded in Building Information Modeling (BIM)', 'automates the extraction of domain semantics and the generation of runtime interfaces', 'integrate Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange', 'standardized extension points to enable modular, service-oriented DT platforms'.

The extraction correctly states 'Not in Document'. While the text mentions 'actuators' as components to be modeled within the BIM environment ('define sensors, actuators, and controllers directly within the BIM environment'), it does not describe the physical actuators themselves, their types, ranges, or limitations as required by the characteristic definition.

The extraction correctly states 'Not in Document'. The text references 'sensor configurations' and 'sensor data' ('post-occupancy sensor data') primarily as inputs or modeling targets, but it lacks specific details about sensor types, measurement capabilities, spatial distribution, or sampling frequencies.

The extraction correctly states 'Not in Document'. Although the abstract mentions using the 'Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric' to evaluate reflection and the text mentions 'validating the model', these are brief references. The text does not detail model fidelity requirements, uncertainty sources, error detection mechanisms, or comprehensive quality assurance processes required by the characteristic definition.

block_4_docs_retrieved:
Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

block_6_docs_retrieved:
[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service

While it mentions that MDE 'strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies' broadly, it does not describe specific data ownership policies, privacy protection measures, consent management, or data sharing agreements for the Digital Twin.

The provided text outlines the system architecture, real-time connectivity, and control logic. It references 'standard compliance' for open-source frameworks and 'regulatory mandates' for BIM. However, it lacks details on specific cybersecurity protocols, access control mechanisms, authentication systems, or safety-critical fail-safe mechanisms implemented in the system.
